title: Nanoparticles — when size changes everything
examTip: The surface area to volume ratio is the key concept. Every unusual property of nanoparticles — increased reactivity, different colour, antibacterial activity — traces back to the huge proportion of atoms on the surface.
introTips: Gold is yellow. Silver is shiny. Titanium dioxide is white. But change any of these to nanoparticle size and their colour, reactivity and properties change completely.
At the nanoscale, a material doesn't just behave differently — it can feel like a completely different substance.
This topic links physics, chemistry and biology — and it raises real ethical questions about safety and regulation.
intro: What if making something smaller didn't just make it a smaller version of itself, but turned it into something with completely different properties? That sounds like science fiction — but it's what happens at the nanoscale. Gold nanoparticles aren't yellow — they're red or purple. Silver nanoparticles kill bacteria with extraordinary efficiency. Titanium dioxide, which is white in paint, becomes transparent in sunscreen. The reason for all of this is one idea: surface area to volume ratio. When a material is cut down to nanometre scale, the proportion of its atoms sitting on the surface explodes — and surface atoms behave very differently from those buried inside. This is nanotechnology, and it's already in products you use every day.

keyPoints.heading: What nanoparticles are
keyPoints.content: Nanoparticles are particles with sizes between 1 and 100 nanometres (nm). One nanometre = 1 × 10⁻⁹ m (one billionth of a metre). Each nanoparticle contains a few hundred to a few thousand atoms. To put this in context: a single atom is approximately 0.1 nm across; a nanoparticle is 1–100 nm; a fine atmospheric particle (PM2.5) is about 2500 nm; a human hair is about 80,000 nm wide. The crucial consequence of being this small is that the surface area to volume ratio becomes enormous. Imagine cutting a cube into smaller and smaller pieces — the total volume stays the same, but the total surface area increases dramatically. At nanometre scale, a very high proportion of all the atoms in a particle are on its surface. Surface atoms behave differently from interior atoms — they have unsatisfied bonds and can interact with their environment. This is why nanoparticles can have completely different properties from the same material in bulk (large-scale) form: different colour, different melting point, different reactivity, different optical properties.
writeBullets: Nanoparticles: 1–100 nm in size (1 nm = 1×10⁻⁹ m); a few hundred to a few thousand atoms
Size comparison: atom ~0.1 nm → nanoparticle 1–100 nm → fine particle ~2500 nm → hair ~80,000 nm
Tiny size → enormous surface area to volume ratio
High proportion of atoms on the surface → surface atoms more reactive (unsatisfied bonds)
Different properties from bulk material: colour, reactivity, melting point
Same substance, same atoms — but nanoscale size changes behaviour completely

keyTerms.def: A particle between 1 and 100 nm in size, containing a few hundred to a few thousand atoms
A unit of length equal to 1×10⁻⁹ m (one billionth of a metre)
The ratio of a particle's surface area to its total volume; increases dramatically as particle size decreases

keyPoints.memTip: Think of cutting up a block of cheese: 1 block has little surface. Cut it into tiny cubes and suddenly there's surface everywhere. Nanoparticles are like the cheese cut into millions of microscopic pieces — mostly surface.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Surface area to volume ratio is the core concept of this whole topic. If Mabel can explain this clearly, she can explain every property and every use that follows.

keyPoints.heading: Properties of nanoparticles
keyPoints.content: The most important property change is greatly increased reactivity — because so many atoms are on the surface, nanoparticles present much more reactive surface area to their surroundings. This makes them far more chemically active than the same material in bulk. Colour changes: gold nanoparticles appear red or purple rather than the yellow of bulk gold — quantum effects at the nanoscale change how they interact with light. Silver nanoparticles have a blue-grey colour. These optical changes are caused by electrons on the surface interacting with light in a fundamentally different way at this scale. Antibacterial activity: silver nanoparticles are powerfully antibacterial — their huge surface area maximises contact with bacterial cells, disrupting their membranes and enzymes. Bulk silver has weak antibacterial properties by comparison. Transparency: titanium dioxide (TiO₂) nanoparticles are transparent to visible light (used in clear sunscreens), while bulk TiO₂ is white (used in white paint). Carbon nanotubes: cylindrical fullerene structures made of rolled graphene. Incredibly strong — stronger than steel per unit weight — and excellent electrical conductors. These properties arise because the carbon-carbon bonds within the nanotube walls are the same as those in graphene.
writeBullets: Very high surface area to volume ratio → much more reactive than bulk material
Gold nanoparticles: red/purple colour (vs yellow bulk gold) — quantum optical effects
Silver nanoparticles: strongly antibacterial (huge surface area in contact with bacteria)
TiO₂ nanoparticles: transparent (sunscreen); bulk TiO₂: white (paint)
Carbon nanotubes: cylindrical fullerenes; stronger than steel; excellent conductors
Properties change because of surface effects and quantum effects at nanoscale

keyTerms.def: A cylindrical form of carbon (rolled graphene); extremely strong and an excellent electrical conductor
A physical behaviour that only occurs at very small scales (nanometres), causing properties like colour to differ from bulk material

keyPoints.memTip: SILVER = antibacterial (kills bacteria). TiO₂ small = CLEAR (sunscreen). TiO₂ big = WHITE (paint). Gold nano = RED/PURPLE. All because surface area to volume ratio is huge at nano scale.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Students often struggle to explain WHY nanoparticles are more reactive. The answer is always: 'higher surface area to volume ratio → more surface atoms available to react'. Keep bringing it back to this.

keyPoints.heading: Uses of nanoparticles
keyPoints.content: Medicine and drug delivery: nanoparticles can be engineered to carry drug molecules and deliver them directly to specific cells (e.g. cancer cells), reducing side effects compared to conventional drugs that affect the whole body. They can cross biological barriers that larger particles cannot. Antibacterial applications: silver nanoparticles are used in wound dressings (large surface area → highly antibacterial), hospital surface coatings, and odour-resistant socks and sportswear. Sunscreens: TiO₂ and ZnO nanoparticles block UV radiation effectively but are transparent to visible light — so they provide UV protection without the white appearance of traditional sun creams. Electronics: carbon nanotubes are potential transistors for ultra-miniaturised computers; silver nanoparticles are used in conductive inks for printed circuits. Cosmetics: nanoparticles in moisturisers, foundations and anti-ageing creams for better skin penetration and texture. Catalysts: nanoparticles of metals such as platinum make extremely efficient catalysts because their surface area maximises the area available for chemical reactions — used in catalytic converters and industrial processes. Self-cleaning coatings: TiO₂ nanoparticles on glass surfaces catalyse the breakdown of organic dirt under UV light; ZnO nanoparticles create water-repellent (hydrophobic) fabric coatings.
writeBullets: Drug delivery: nanoparticles carry drugs directly to cancer cells; reduce side effects
Silver nanoparticles: wound dressings, hospital coatings, antibacterial sportswear
Sunscreen: TiO₂/ZnO nanoparticles block UV but are transparent to visible light
Electronics: carbon nanotubes as transistors; silver nanoparticles in conductive inks
Catalysts: high surface area makes nanoparticle catalysts extremely efficient
Self-cleaning glass: TiO₂ nanoparticles break down dirt using UV light

keyTerms.def: Using nanoparticles to carry drugs to specific cells (e.g. cancer cells), reducing side effects
A substance that speeds up a chemical reaction without being used up; nanoparticle catalysts are highly effective due to enormous surface area

keyPoints.memTip: Think about where nanoparticles appear in your life: sunscreen (TiO₂), hospital coatings (Ag), catalytic converters (Pt). They're already everywhere — each use exploits that huge surface area.
keyPoints.tutor_note: AQA often asks students to suggest a use of nanoparticles and explain WHY they're effective for that use. The answer always links back to high surface area to volume ratio.

keyPoints.heading: Risks and the ethics of nanoparticles
keyPoints.content: The same properties that make nanoparticles useful also create potential risks. Because they are so small, nanoparticles can penetrate biological barriers that larger particles cannot: they can enter cells, cross the blood-brain barrier, and be inhaled deep into the lungs (reaching alveoli). This raises serious concerns about toxicity. Many nanoparticles are relatively new — their long-term effects on human health and ecosystems are not yet fully understood. Regulation is difficult — nanoparticles are hard to detect and monitor in the environment. The same high reactivity that makes them effective catalysts or antibacterial agents could mean they react with and damage biological molecules inside cells. Scientific evidence is mixed: some nanoparticles appear safe in tests; others show toxic effects in laboratory studies. A historical parallel: asbestos was once thought safe and widely used — its risks only became clear decades later, after enormous harm was done. This is a reminder that 'we don't yet know the risks' is not the same as 'it is safe'. Society must weigh the proven benefits of nanotechnology against uncertain but potentially serious risks — this requires careful regulation, precautionary approaches, and ongoing research. This is an example of the tension between scientific innovation and the need for safety evidence.
writeBullets: Small size → can penetrate cell membranes, cross blood-brain barrier, reach deep lung
Long-term health effects largely unknown — many nanoparticles are new materials
Regulation difficult: hard to detect and monitor nanoparticles in the environment
High reactivity (useful for catalysis) could damage biological molecules in cells
Historical parallel: asbestos was once thought safe — risks emerged decades later
Risk vs benefit: balance proven uses against uncertain but potentially serious dangers

keyTerms.def: A protective membrane that prevents most large particles from passing from the blood into the brain; nanoparticles can cross it
The idea that if an action has suspected risks that are not yet fully understood, caution should be taken before widespread use

keyPoints.memTip: SMALL = can go ANYWHERE in the body. The same feature that makes nanoparticles useful for drug delivery (crossing barriers) is the same feature that makes them potentially dangerous.
keyPoints.tutor_note: This is one of the best topics in GCSE chemistry for 'How Science Works' and ethics questions. Mabel should be able to write a balanced argument: benefits (real, proven) vs risks (uncertain, but potentially serious). Neither side is 'wrong' — both require evidence.

tables.title: Nanoparticle applications — material, property exploited, use, and potential risk
rows: Toxicity to human cells at high concentrations; environmental harm to aquatic organisms
Unknown long-term effects if nanoparticles penetrate skin
Biocompatible; can be functionalised with drugs
Lung damage if inhaled (similar concerns to asbestos fibres)
Unknown effects if nanoparticles enter waterways

0.question: Which of the following best explains why nanoparticles are more reactive than the same material in bulk form?
options: They have a much higher surface area to volume ratio, so more atoms are exposed on the surface and available to react
They contain more electrons than bulk material, making them more chemically active
Their atoms are arranged in a different crystal structure at the nanoscale
They have a higher temperature due to their small size, increasing reaction rate
0.correctFeedback: Exactly right! The surface area to volume ratio is the key. As particle size decreases, a much higher proportion of atoms are on the surface — and surface atoms are the ones that participate in chemical reactions. So nanoparticles have far more reactive surface per unit mass than the same material in bulk form.
0.wrongFeedback: The key is the surface area to volume ratio. Nanoparticles don't have more electrons or a higher temperature — but they do have an enormous proportion of their atoms sitting on the surface, where reactions occur. More surface atoms = more reactive.

2.question: Silver nanoparticles are used in wound dressings because they are strongly antibacterial. Which property of nanoparticles explains this?
options: Their very high surface area to volume ratio maximises contact between silver and bacteria
Silver nanoparticles contain more silver atoms than bulk silver per gram
Nanoparticles travel through the body faster and reach wounds more quickly
Silver nanoparticles are transparent and allow doctors to monitor wound healing
2.correctFeedback: Correct! The huge surface area to volume ratio means that silver nanoparticles have an enormous surface area in contact with bacteria — maximising the antibacterial effect. Bulk silver has the same number of silver atoms per gram but far less surface area exposed.
2.wrongFeedback: It's not about travelling faster or transparency. The key is that nanoparticles have a much higher surface area to volume ratio — this means a far greater proportion of silver atoms are on the surface and in direct contact with bacteria, making the antibacterial effect much stronger than bulk silver.

3.question: A student says 'nanoparticles in sunscreen must be safe because we've been using sunscreen for decades.' What is the flaw in this reasoning?
options: Nanoparticle sunscreens are relatively new; long-term health effects are not yet known and insufficient time has passed to detect them
Sunscreen is not tested before it goes on sale, so safety cannot be assumed
Nanoparticles in sunscreen are too small to be tested by scientists
Traditional sunscreens did not contain nanoparticles; only recent formulations do
3.correctFeedback: Good critical thinking! Nanoparticle formulations in sunscreen are relatively recent technology. The fact that traditional sunscreen is safe doesn't mean nanoparticle versions are — the nanoparticles themselves may behave differently in the body. Long-term studies take decades, and the historical example of asbestos shows that 'no evidence of harm yet' is not the same as 'safe'.
3.wrongFeedback: The flaw is about timeframe and novelty. Nanoparticle sunscreens are a relatively recent development — even if traditional sunscreens have been around for decades, the nanoparticle versions haven't. Long-term effects take time to detect, and history (e.g. asbestos) shows that apparent safety in the short term doesn't guarantee long-term safety.

commonMistakes: Saying nanoparticles are 'more reactive because they are smaller' without explaining WHY — you must link small size to high surface area to volume ratio, and that to more surface atoms available to react.
Confusing surface area to volume ratio with total surface area — nanoparticles have a high RATIO, not necessarily more total surface area than a large piece of the same material.
Saying all nanoparticles are dangerous — this is not supported by evidence. Some appear safe; others show risks. The issue is uncertainty, not proven danger across the board.
Forgetting that the same property (e.g. ability to cross biological barriers) is both what makes nanoparticles useful (drug delivery) and what makes them potentially risky (entering unwanted cells).
Confusing fullerenes and nanoparticles — fullerenes (C₆₀, carbon nanotubes) are a specific type of carbon nanoparticle; nanoparticles in general can be made of any material.
examTips: For any nanoparticle property question, ask yourself: 'how does this link to surface area to volume ratio?' Almost every answer does.

When asked about risks of nanoparticles, always include: small size → penetrate biological barriers; long-term effects unknown; regulation is difficult. Three separate points for three separate marks.
For 'evaluate the use of nanoparticles' questions, structure your answer: benefits (specific, with examples) → risks (specific, with evidence) → conclusion. AQA rewards balanced arguments.
Make sure you can give the correct scale: 1 nm = 1×10⁻⁹ m. If the question gives a size in nm, check whether it falls in the 1–100 nm nanoparticle range.
revisionCardBullets: Nanoparticles: 1–100 nm; 1 nm = 1×10⁻⁹ m; a few hundred to a few thousand atoms
Tiny size → huge surface area to volume ratio → more surface atoms → more reactive
Gold nanoparticles: red/purple (not yellow like bulk gold) — quantum optical effects
Silver nanoparticles: strongly antibacterial; wound dressings, hospital surfaces
TiO₂ nanoparticles: transparent (sunscreen); bulk TiO₂: white (paint)
Carbon nanotubes: cylindrical fullerenes; stronger than steel; excellent conductors
Uses: drug delivery, antibacterial coatings, sunscreen, electronics, self-cleaning glass
Drug delivery: nanoparticles carry drugs to specific cells; reduce side effects
Catalysts: nanoparticle catalysts very efficient due to huge surface area
Risk: small size → cross biological barriers; enter cells; cross blood-brain barrier
Long-term effects unknown; regulation difficult; asbestos as historical warning
Balance benefits (real, proven) vs risks (uncertain but potentially serious)
